symptom: Weak cooling, supply vents noticeably warmer than usual. Ice visible on the suction (larger) copper line near the outdoor unit. Compressor runs continuously without satisfying thermostat.
diagnosis: Low refrigerant charge, almost always caused by a leak in the evaporator coil, line set, or service valve. Running low refrigerant damages the compressor — must not be topped off without leak search.
procedure: Confirm with gauge set: measure suction and liquid line pressures, compare against charge-by-superheat / subcooling targets on the unit's nameplate.
Inspect the visible line set for oil traces — refrigerant carries oil, so any leak leaves a residue.
Electronic leak detector sweep at the service valves, coil U-bends, and line-set flare fittings.
If coil leak confirmed: quote coil replacement OR full system replacement based on age.
If line-set leak: braze repair, pressure test with nitrogen, evacuate to <500 microns, recharge by weight.
Do NOT top off without finding the leak — EPA violation and the problem returns in weeks.
After repair: run the system and confirm superheat/subcooling back in spec.
safety_notes: R410A is high-pressure (~400 psi). Gauges, hoses, and recovery tank must be rated for it.
EPA Section 608 certification required for any refrigerant work.
Ice on the suction line means the coil is below freezing — do not disconnect lines until fully thawed, or you'll get a liquid slug.
